runningGuidePage.pageDesc: A guide to configuring operational limits and running variables for the simulation.
runningGuidePage.overviewDesc: The Running Parameter Page is used to set the operational limits like braking forces, speed restrictions, and passenger loads. You need to configure this page before running dynamic or static simulations.
runningGuidePage.inputFieldsDesc: Each field represents a specific operational variable. Enter a numeric value directly in the box, or select a predefined gear (e.g., P1 to P7, B1 to B7). All fields are validated — values must be non-negative and within their allowed ranges. The simulation uses these values to calculate tractive and braking effort.
runningGuidePage.btnSaveDesc: Validates and sends the current form values to the simulation engine. You must save before running the simulation.
runningGuidePage.btnResetDesc: Clears all saved form data and restores default values. Use this to start fresh.
runningGuidePage.btnExportDesc: Downloads the current form values as a CSV file. This file can later be imported.
runningGuidePage.btnUploadDesc: Opens a file picker. Select an exported CSV file to automatically fill all matching fields.
runningGuidePage.csvTemplateDesc: Not sure about the expected CSV format? Navigate to Templates → Running Parameter in the sidebar to download a ready-made template file with all field names pre-filled. Load the template, fill in your values, then use Upload CSV to import it.

electricalGuidePage.pageDesc: A guide to configuring the train's electrical systems, voltage, and efficiency metrics.
electricalGuidePage.overviewDesc: The Electrical Parameter Page allows you to specify power limits, efficiencies for components like gearboxes and traction motors, and auxiliary power consumption. Like Track Parameters, you can input static values or upload dynamic CSV data.
electricalGuidePage.inputFieldsDesc: Use these fields to set a single, constant electrical value (e.g., constant line voltage or constant motor efficiency) for the entire journey.
electricalGuidePage.inputUploadDesc: For electrical properties that change over time or distance, click the upload icon next to the field to attach a CSV file. The simulation engine will prioritize this data over the static value.
electricalGuidePage.btnSaveDesc: Validates and applies your electrical parameters to the simulation engine.
electricalGuidePage.btnResetDesc: Clears all static fields and dynamic uploads, restoring the factory default electrical settings.
electricalGuidePage.btnExportDesc: Downloads your current static electrical form values into a CSV file.
electricalGuidePage.btnUploadDesc: Upload a previously exported CSV to instantly populate the static input fields on this page.
electricalGuidePage.csvTemplateDesc: Need a template for importing static electrical values? Navigate to Templates → Electrical Parameter in the sidebar.

trackGuidePage.pageDesc: A guide to configuring the physical track layout, such as stations, distances, and speed limits.
trackGuidePage.overviewDesc: The Track Parameter Page allows you to define the infrastructure properties of the railway line. You can configure static variables manually, or upload CSV files for dynamic simulation where parameters like slopes or speed limits change over distance.
trackGuidePage.inputFieldsDesc: Use these fields to set a single, constant value for the entire track (e.g., a constant gradient or constant speed limit). If a dynamic CSV file is uploaded for a variable, it overrides the static input.
trackGuidePage.inputUploadDesc: For variables that change over distance (like radius, slope, or speed limit), click the upload icon next to the field to attach a CSV file. The simulation will use this distance-based data instead of the static value.
trackGuidePage.btnSaveDesc: Validates and applies your track parameters and uploaded files to the simulation engine.
trackGuidePage.btnResetDesc: Clears all static fields and removes all attached dynamic CSV files, restoring factory defaults.
trackGuidePage.btnExportDesc: Downloads your current static form values into a CSV file. (Note: this exports the single-value settings, not the dynamic files).
trackGuidePage.btnUploadDesc: Upload a previously exported CSV to instantly populate the static input fields on this form.
trackGuidePage.csvTemplateDesc: Need a template for importing static track values? Navigate to Templates → Track Parameter in the sidebar.
trackGuidePage.slopeOptionsDesc: This secondary panel allows you to define specific gradient profiles or preset options used by the simulation's track calculation algorithm. It functions similarly to the main form, complete with its own dedicated Save, Reset, Upload, and Export buttons for managing the presets independently.

outputGuidePage.pageDesc: A guide to understanding and exporting simulation results.
outputGuidePage.overviewDesc: The Output Page visualizes your simulation data. Depending on your current simulation state, you will either see an empty state prompting you to run a simulation, or a detailed breakdown of results.
outputGuidePage.emptyStateDesc: You haven't run any simulation yet. Please navigate to the sidebar and start either a Static or Dynamic simulation first.
outputGuidePage.runStaticDesc: Click this button in the sidebar to simulate train behavior based strictly on your static input values. This ignores any uploaded CSV files.
outputGuidePage.runDynamicDesc: Click this button in the sidebar to simulate behavior based on uploaded CSV files. Variables change dynamically over distance or speed.
outputGuidePage.summaryDesc: These cards show a quick overview of the simulation's peak values, total travel distance, energy consumed, and max speed.
outputGuidePage.tabsDesc: Switch between these tabs to visualize different performance metrics over time or distance.
outputGuidePage.distanceTabDesc: In the Distance tab, static simulations will show a smooth theoretical curve. Dynamic simulations will overlay discrete markers or steps depending on your CSV data granularity.
outputGuidePage.saveChartDesc: Click here to download the currently visible chart as a PNG image file.
outputGuidePage.saveChartCsvDesc: Downloads only the data points from the currently visible chart as a CSV file.
outputGuidePage.saveChartExcelDesc: Downloads only the data points from the currently visible chart as an XLSX file.
outputGuidePage.exportAllDesc: Use these buttons to download the entire simulation dataset (all variables) into a single comprehensive CSV or Excel file. A toast notification will pop up showing the exact file path where it was saved.

optimizationGuidePage.pageDesc: A guide to exploring and evaluating multiple simulation parameters using fuzzy logic optimization.
optimizationGuidePage.overviewDesc: The Optimization Page runs a parameter sweep (evaluating all combinations of Acceleration and Weakening Point within your specified ranges) and ranks them using a Mamdani fuzzy logic engine based on travel time, peak motor power, and energy consumption.
optimizationGuidePage.emptyStateDesc: If you haven't run an optimization yet, you need to define your search space and start the process.
optimizationGuidePage.searchSpaceDesc: Define the minimum and maximum sweep ranges for Acceleration and Weakening Point. The system will simulate every combination.
optimizationGuidePage.startDesc: Click this button to begin evaluating all combinations. A progress bar will show the live status.
optimizationGuidePage.resultsStateDesc: Once the optimization finishes, you can explore the best combinations and analyze how the fuzzy logic algorithm graded them.
optimizationGuidePage.tabsDesc: Switch between viewing the Winner Analysis (ranking and extremes) and the raw Fuzzy Membership Chart (showing the fuzzy sets and scores).
optimizationGuidePage.topBottomDesc: Quickly see which parameters performed the best (highest fuzzy score) and worst overall in the Winner Analysis tab.
optimizationGuidePage.extremesDesc: Also in the Winner Analysis tab, see the fastest, slowest, most powerful, and most efficient individual runs.
optimizationGuidePage.fuzzyTabDesc: This tab visualizes the input (Time, Power, Energy) and output (Score) membership functions for the winner, showing exactly how the fuzzy logic engine graded it against the established curves.
optimizationGuidePage.fuzzyTimeDesc: This chart graphs the input membership for Travel Time. Shorter times intersect the 'Short' curve, gaining a higher membership degree, while longer times fall into 'Medium' or 'Long'.
optimizationGuidePage.fuzzyPowerDesc: This chart maps the motor's Peak Power into 'Low', 'Medium', and 'High' categories. The lower the power, the higher the 'Low' membership degree, reducing infrastructure strain.
optimizationGuidePage.fuzzyEnergyDesc: This chart plots total Energy Consumption. Highly efficient runs will maximize their membership in the 'Low' category, improving the final output score.
optimizationGuidePage.fuzzyScoreDesc: Finally, this chart shows how the rules engine combines the previous inputs to calculate the overall Score (0-100), categorized into 'Poor', 'Fair', 'Good', or 'Excellent'.
optimizationGuidePage.metricTimeDesc: The total duration (in seconds) the train takes to travel from the start to the end of the track. A lower travel time generally contributes to a higher fuzzy score (faster is better).
optimizationGuidePage.metricPowerDesc: The maximum power (in kW) drawn by a single motor during the trip. Lower peak power is preferred to reduce infrastructure stress and improve the fuzzy score.
optimizationGuidePage.metricEnergyDesc: The total electrical energy (in kWh) consumed throughout the entire journey. Lower energy consumption is highly favorable for the fuzzy engine's efficiency rating.
optimizationGuidePage.metricScoreDesc: The final aggregated score (0-100) calculated by the Mamdani fuzzy logic engine, defuzzified using the centroid method. It balances speed, power, and efficiency to rank the overall quality of the run.
optimizationGuidePage.exportCsvDesc: Use the 'Save Results CSV' button at the top to download the entire set of evaluated combinations and their scores.

trainGuidePage.pageDesc: A step-by-step guide to configuring all train-specific inputs before running a simulation.
trainGuidePage.overviewDesc1: The Train Parameter Page is split into two independent panels that must both be saved before a simulation can be started:
trainGuidePage.overviewDesc2: Physical and geometric constants of the rolling stock (inertial coefficients, axle count, wheel diameter, gear ratio, load condition, etc.).
trainGuidePage.overviewDesc3: The composition of the trainset — how many of each car type exist, how many passengers each carries, and the tare mass of each car type.
trainGuidePage.inputFieldsDesc: Each field in this panel represents a fixed physical property of the train. Enter a numeric value directly in the box. All fields are validated — values must be non-negative and within the allowed range (max 5,000 for most fields). A red error message appears beneath the field if the value is invalid.
trainGuidePage.btnSaveDesc: Validates and sends the current form values to the simulation engine. You must save before any simulation results will reflect your changes. The button shows a spinner while the request is in progress.
trainGuidePage.btnResetDesc: Clears all saved form data and restores default factory values. Use this to start fresh if your current configuration is inconsistent.
trainGuidePage.btnExportDesc: Downloads the current form values as a key-value CSV file (e.g. train-constant-parameters.csv). This file can later be re-imported to restore the exact same configuration.
trainGuidePage.btnUploadDesc: Opens a file picker. Select a previously exported CSV file to auto-populate all matching fields. Unrecognised columns are silently skipped; a toast notification counts how many fields were updated.
trainGuidePage.csvTemplateDesc1: Not sure about the expected CSV format? Navigate to Templates → Train Parameter in the sidebar to download a ready-made template file with all field names pre-filled. Load the template, fill in your values, then use Upload CSV to import it.
trainGuidePage.nCarDesc: The n_car dropdown is the central control for this panel. Selecting a configuration (e.g. 12, 10, 8, 6, 4, or the degraded variants 12-Degraded / 10-Degraded) immediately updates the live train illustration displayed next to the dropdown. The illustration gives an instant visual confirmation of the selected consist length and car arrangement. The degraded modes represent a trainset operating with one motor car out of service.
trainGuidePage.compTitle: Car Count, Passenger Capacity & Mass Fields
trainGuidePage.compDesc: Below the selector, three sub-tables allow you to specify the exact properties for each car type (M1, M2, Tc, T1, T2, T3):
trainGuidePage.calcMassDesc: After saving, the app automatically computes three derived values shown in a read-only card: Empty Mass (tare mass × car counts), Loaded Mass (empty + passenger load at selected AW condition), and Inertial Mass (loaded mass factored by inertial coefficients). These values feed directly into the simulation force equations.
trainGuidePage.tsActionDesc: This panel shares the same button set as the Constant Parameters panel: Save Trainset sends the configuration, Reset restores defaults, Export CSV saves the current values to train-trainset-parameters.csv, and Upload CSV allows re-importing a previously exported file.
trainGuidePage.tsTemplateDesc: A ready-made trainset parameter template is available at Templates → Train Parameter in the sidebar. Download it, fill in the car counts, passenger capacities, and masses, then import it using Upload CSV to populate all fields in one step.
trainGuidePage.desc_n_axle: Total number of axles per car
trainGuidePage.desc_n_tm: Number of traction motors per motor car
trainGuidePage.desc_gearRatio: Gear ratio of the traction gearbox
trainGuidePage.desc_carLength: Length of a single car body
trainGuidePage.desc_loadCondition: Passenger load level: AW0 (empty) → AW4 (crush load)
trainGuidePage.desc_carNum: Number of cars of each type in the consist, including degraded motor cars
trainGuidePage.desc_passCap: Maximum passenger count per car type
trainGuidePage.desc_carMass: Tare (empty) mass of each car type in tons

common.inputsDesc: Fill out these parameters manually, or use the file upload option if you have a CSV.
common.actionsDesc: Use these buttons to Upload a CSV, Export your current settings, Reset the form, or Save your changes.

track.title: Track Parameters
track.description: Configure the physical track layout, such as slopes, friction, and distances.

running.title: Running Parameter Page
running.description: This page sets the operational limits like braking forces, speed restrictions, and passenger loads.
running.inputsDesc: Enter numeric values or select predefined gears. These parameters control tractive and braking efforts during the simulation.
running.actionsDesc: Use these to Save your configuration, Reset to defaults, or Export/Upload CSV files.
running.templateDesc: Need a template? Click Template above or go to Templates → Running Parameter in the sidebar.

electrical.title: Electrical Parameters
electrical.description: Define voltage limits, resistance, and the number of operational motors.

train.constantInputsDesc: Enter and adjust your desired physical variable values here. All fields are validated and must be non-negative.
train.constantSaveDesc: Click Save to validate and send your inputs to the simulation engine. Always save before running a simulation.
train.constantResetDesc: Click Reset to restore factory defaults and clear saved data.
train.constantUploadDesc: Click Upload CSV to import a previously exported file and populate all fields instantly.
train.constantExportDesc: Click Export CSV to download your current values as a file.
train.constantTemplateDesc: Click this button to go to the Templates page and get a ready-made CSV template.
train.trainsetNCarDesc: Select the number of cars in your consist here. This value individually changes the live train illustration next to it.
train.trainsetInputsDesc: Adjust the specific variable values for car counts, passenger capacities, and tare masses across different car types.
train.trainsetSaveDesc: Click Save to validate and send your trainset inputs.
train.trainsetResetDesc: Click Reset to restore trainset factory defaults.
train.trainsetUploadDesc: Click Upload CSV to populate these trainset fields instantly from a file.
train.trainsetExportDesc: Click Export CSV to download the trainset configuration.
train.trainsetTemplateDesc: Click this button to go to the Templates page and get a ready-made CSV template for trainset parameters.

topbar.title: Simulation Controls & Top Bar
topbar.description: Learn about the simulation execution controls and other utility functions in the top bar.
topbar.staticBtnDesc: Runs a static validation sweep simulating raw train capabilities over a series of speeds. All environmental parameters (slope, radius, etc.) are forced to 0.
topbar.dynamicBtnDesc: Runs a full dynamic simulation, incorporating real-world track geometry data such as gradients, curves, and speed limits over the route.
topbar.localeBtnDesc: Switches the user interface and guide language between English (EN) and Bahasa Indonesia (ID) instantly without page reload.
topbar.docsBtnDesc: Opens the comprehensive documentation containing technical details about the simulation engine, system architecture, and fuzzy logic calculations.
topbar.themeBtnDesc: Toggles the application appearance between Light, Dark, or System theme settings.

warnings.WARN_STAT_VOL_MOTOR_ZERO: Static Motor Voltage is 0 or not set!

warnings.WARN_N_STATION_ZERO: Number of stations is 0 or not set!
warnings.WARN_X_STATION_ZERO: Station distance is 0 or not set!

results.tabsDesc: Switch between various data visualizations to analyze power, speed, distance, and more.
results.exportDesc: You can export the raw simulation data to CSV or Excel for external analysis.

Optimization.description: Parameter sweep for each combinations, scored by Mamdani fuzzy logic.
Optimization.searchSpaceDescription: Define the minimum and maximum sweep ranges for optimization.
Optimization.constraintsWarning: Warning, your constraints are too tight, consider adjusting your constraints or adjusting acceleration and weakening point to provide the best result.
Optimization.noResultsDescription: Click Start Optimization to run the full fuzzy parameter sweep across all acc/v_p1 step combinations.

toast.busyDescription: Unable to start optimization while a simulation is running. Please wait until it finishes.

Optimization.outputMembershipDesc: Defuzzified output (Centroid) from Time, Power, and Energy engines. The final score is the average of TimeScore, PowerScore, and EnergyScore.
Optimization.outputVariable: Output variable: Poor (0) → Excellent (100). Aggregated using MAX.

DocsIntro.title: Introduction
DocsIntro.subtitle: Welcome to the PT INKA Train Simulation App technical manual. This documentation provides a comprehensive guide for engineering staff to understand, operate, and analyze train physics simulations.
DocsIntro.whatIsDesc1: The Train Simulation App is a high-performance desktop application designed to model the longitudinal dynamics of rolling stock. By inputting specific mechanical, aerodynamic, electrical, and track geometry parameters, engineers can accurately predict the behavior of a train over a given journey.
DocsIntro.whatIsDesc2: This tool empowers PT INKA to validate traction motor specifications, estimate energy consumption, and optimize operating parameters before physical prototypes are ever placed on the track.
DocsIntro.featPhysicsDesc: Simulates resistance, traction, and braking forces at a highly granular 0.1s time-step, factoring in complex environments like tunnels and gradients.
DocsIntro.featFuzzyDesc: Automatically sweeps parameter spaces (like acceleration limits) to find the absolute Pareto-optimal balance between travel time, energy, and motor stress.
DocsIntro.featAnalyticsDesc: Provides rich, interactive charts and tables that plot kinematic and electrical profiles across distance and time, ready for CSV/Excel export.
DocsIntro.workflowDesc: The application follows a strict sequential workflow to ensure accurate analysis:
DocsIntro.wf1Desc: Configure the train parameters, running limits, track geometry, and electrical specifications. You can input these values manually via the form fields or quickly populate them by uploading pre-formatted .csv templates. Every parameter page must be saved before proceeding.
DocsIntro.wf2Desc: Run either a Static or Dynamic simulation from the top navigation bar. This establishes the baseline performance profile (speed, power, and energy consumption) for your current configuration.
DocsIntro.wf3Desc: Navigate to the Optimization page. Here, you define the lower and upper bounds for the Acceleration Limit and Weakening Point. The system will generate a matrix of all possible combinations within this range.
DocsIntro.wf4Desc: Initiate the optimization process. The C++ engine simulates every parameter combination in the evaluation space, grading each run using the Mamdani Fuzzy Inference System based on Travel Time, Peak Power, and Energy Efficiency.
DocsIntro.wf5Desc: Review the optimization results. The system automatically identifies the absolute best parameter combination (The Winner). You can analyze the Top 5 / Bottom 5 runs, inspect the metric extremes, and view exactly how the fuzzy logic engine scored the winner via the interactive Fuzzy Membership Chart.
DocsIntro.navDesc: Use the sidebar to explore the deep technical workings of the simulation:
DocsIntro.navArch: Understand the C++ and Qt WebEngine hybrid layer.
DocsIntro.navMech: Dive into the Davis equation and traction calculations.
DocsIntro.navFuzzy: See how the optimization scoring works mathematically.

DocsArch.title: System Architecture
DocsArch.subtitle: A deep dive into the hybrid software architecture bridging a high-performance C++ backend with a reactive web frontend.
DocsArch.hybridDesc1: The Train Simulation Desktop Application is not a standard web app. It runs locally as a desktop executable powered by the Qt framework. The interface you see is a modern Single Page Application (SPA) built with React/Next.js, hosted entirely inside a native QWebEngineView.
DocsArch.hybridDesc2: This architecture offers the best of both worlds: the rich, modern, and rapid UI development of web technologies combined with the raw computational speed and low-level memory control of C++ necessary for heavy physics loops.
DocsArch.ipcDesc: Because the UI runs in a JavaScript engine and the physics core runs in native C++, they communicate using a dual-channel approach: HTTP REST for heavy payload transmission and Qt WebChannel for native integrations.
DocsArch.ipcDetail: When you press Start Simulation, the frontend aggregates the states into a JSON object and POSTs it to the local C++ HTTP Server (localhost:8080/api/*). The ApiHandler intercepts this and instantiates the numerical integration classes.
DocsArch.fileDesc1: Standard web applications are severely restricted by browser sandboxes, meaning they cannot directly read or write files to the user's hard drive without clunky download prompts.
DocsArch.fileDesc2: To overcome this, all CSV importing, CSV exporting, and Excel exporting actions bypass the web engine entirely. The frontend sends a signal over WebChannel to the FileBridge component, and the C++ backend triggers native Windows/Linux OS file dialogs (via QFileDialog). The C++ core handles the disk I/O natively and passes the processed tabular data back to the frontend for charting.

DocsMech.title: Simulation Mechanics
DocsMech.subtitle: Detailed explanation of the kinematic integration loop, environmental resistances, and motor tractive effort processing.
DocsMech.staticDesc: Static mode is a pure validation test. It forces all environmental geometry parameters to zero (0‰ gradient, infinite radius curves, no tunnels). This isolates the rolling stock's raw mechanical and electrical capabilities, ensuring the basic traction motor characteristic curves perform as specified without track-induced drag.
DocsMech.dynamicDesc: Dynamic mode simulates a real-world journey. As the engine integrates distance (odometer), it continuously queries the Track Parameters array. If the train enters a region with a 20‰ uphill gradient or a 500m radius curve, the instantaneous resistance vector spikes, accurately altering acceleration.
DocsMech.resistanceDesc: At every 0.1-second tick, the backend calculates the opposing forces acting against the traction motor. The baseline resistance is modeled using the expanded Davis Equation:
DocsMech.siNote: All units evaluated internally using standard SI variants (N, m/s).
DocsMech.termA: Mechanical Journal Friction: A constant drag coefficient regardless of speed.
DocsMech.termBv: Flange Friction: Drag that scales linearly with velocity (v).
DocsMech.termCv2: Aerodynamic Drag: Scales quadratically with speed. C is derived from the frontal area and the train's drag coefficient.
DocsMech.termRg: Gradient Force: Gravity vector calculated as Mass * g * sin(θ) where θ is the slope angle.
DocsMech.termRc: Curve Resistance: Extra bogie friction induced by tight track radii.
DocsMech.tractiveDesc: The motor cannot supply infinite torque. Tractive effort is strictly bounded by two regions defined in the Electrical Parameters:
DocsMech.tractiveConstant: Constant Torque Region: From 0 km/h up to the Weakening Point. Torque is at maximum, making acceleration dependent only on mass and the configured acceleration limit.
DocsMech.tractiveConstantPower: Constant Power Region: Beyond the Weakening Point. Torque drops inversely proportional to speed, meaning acceleration inherently decays as the train approaches maximum velocity.
DocsMech.tractiveAdhesion: Additionally, an Adhesion Limit check prevents wheel slip. If the requested tractive effort exceeds the friction coefficient between the wheel and the rail (relative to axle load), the tractive effort is hard-capped.

DocsFuzzy.title: Fuzzy Logic Optimization
DocsFuzzy.subtitle: How the Mamdani Inference System evaluates every simulated parameter combination to find the optimal train specification.
DocsFuzzy.problemDesc1: Designing a train specification involves inherently conflicting trade-offs. You cannot tune all three metrics in the same direction at once — increasing acceleration shortens travel time but raises peak motor power and energy consumption. Lowering the weakening point reduces peak power but may slow the train on hills.
DocsFuzzy.problemDesc2: The Mamdani Fuzzy Inference System solves this by grading every simulated combination holistically — the same way a human engineer would — producing a single Optimization Score per run so the best overall combination can be identified objectively.
DocsFuzzy.tradeoff1: Higher acceleration → shorter travel time ✓  but higher peak motor power ✗ and more energy ✗
DocsFuzzy.tradeoff2: Lower weakening point → smaller peak power ✓  but potential speed loss on gradients ✗
DocsFuzzy.tradeoff3: The fuzzy engine finds the combination where all three metrics are best balanced.
DocsFuzzy.howTitle: How the Optimizer Works — Two Passes
DocsFuzzy.howDesc: The optimizer runs in two sequential passes to ensure the fuzzy membership functions are always calibrated to the actual data range for this specific train and track, never relying on hardcoded constants.
DocsFuzzy.pass1Desc: The system builds a grid of Acceleration × Weakening Point combinations centred on your current parameters. It runs a full physics simulation for each combination (up to 20 runs) and records Travel Time, Peak Motor Power, and Energy Consumption for each.
DocsFuzzy.pass2Desc: After all simulations complete, the observed min/max from Pass 1 are used to calibrate the membership functions. Every combination is then evaluated by the fuzzy engine and receives a score from 0 to 100. The highest-scoring combination is The Winner.
DocsFuzzy.howNote: Because membership function boundaries are derived from real Pass 1 data, the same engine works for any train type or route without changing a single constant.
DocsFuzzy.fuzzDesc1: Before the rules can fire, each crisp numerical result (e.g. 3520 s travel time) must be translated into linguistic degrees of membership. The system maps each value onto three overlapping fuzzy sets: Short/Low, Medium, and Long/High.
DocsFuzzy.fuzzDesc2: The boundaries of these sets are not fixed numbers — they are computed at runtime from the actual Pass 1 min and max, with a 5% margin added to both ends so boundary results still receive non-zero membership.
DocsFuzzy.trapezoidDesc: Used for Short/Low and Long/High terms. Has a flat plateau at full membership (1.0) so values deep inside the extreme zones are unambiguously classified, then slopes down toward the centre.
DocsFuzzy.triangleDesc: Used for the Medium term. Peaks at a single midpoint and falls off symmetrically in both directions, allowing smooth partial membership in overlapping transition zones.
DocsFuzzy.fuzzOverlapNote: The Short and Long terms intentionally overlap with Medium in the transition zones (0.30r–0.45r and 0.55r–0.70r), so intermediate values activate multiple terms simultaneously — this is what makes the output smooth and continuous.
DocsFuzzy.enginesDesc: The system uses three separate single-input Mamdani engines. Running them independently allows each metric to be weighted equally in the final score and keeps the rule base simple and auditable — each engine has exactly 3 rules.
DocsFuzzy.engineTimeDesc: Input: Travel Time (seconds). Evaluates how fast the trip is. Short travel time → high TimeScore.
DocsFuzzy.enginePowerDesc: Input: Peak Motor Power (kW). Evaluates how efficiently the motor operates. Low peak power → high PowerScore.
DocsFuzzy.engineEnergyDesc: Input: Energy Consumption (kWh). Evaluates total energy usage for the journey. Low consumption → high EnergyScore.
DocsFuzzy.rulesTitle: Rule Base (3 rules per engine, 9 total)
DocsFuzzy.inferenceDesc1: For each active rule, the engine calculates its firing strength — how strongly the current input satisfies the rule's condition. Since each engine has exactly one input variable, the activation strength equals the membership degree of the input in the antecedent term.
DocsFuzzy.inferenceDesc2: For rules with multiple antecedents (if extended), the firing strength would be the minimum membership across all conditions, implementing the fuzzy AND operator.
DocsFuzzy.inferenceFormula: The firing strength α is the minimum membership across all antecedent terms. A rule with α = 0 does not fire.
DocsFuzzy.aggDesc: Multiple rules can fire simultaneously with different strengths. The output variable's shape is built by sweeping 100 evenly-spaced points across the [0, 100] range. At each point x, the engine takes the maximum clipped membership across all active rules — this is the MAX aggregation operator.
DocsFuzzy.defuzzDesc1: The aggregated output is a fuzzy shape, not a single number. The Centroid method (Center of Gravity) converts it to one crisp value: the balance point of the shape.
DocsFuzzy.defuzzFormula: Discretised Centroid formula (100 integration steps over [0, 100])
DocsFuzzy.defuzzDesc2: Each engine produces one score (TimeScore, PowerScore, EnergyScore) on a 0–100 scale. The output membership functions for all three engines use fixed boundaries: Poor (0–30), Fair (20–55), Good (45–78), Excellent (68–100).
DocsFuzzy.scoreExcellent: Short travel time and/or low motor power / energy — an ideal combination.
DocsFuzzy.scoreGood: One metric is excellent, the other is acceptable — still a strong result.
DocsFuzzy.scoreFair: At least one metric is mediocre — acceptable but not recommended.
DocsFuzzy.scorePoor: Slow trip or very high power draw (or both) — this combination is not optimal.
DocsFuzzy.finalScoreDesc: The three engine scores are averaged into a single Optimization Score. This equal weighting reflects the engineering philosophy that a good train specification must balance time efficiency, motor durability, and energy cost simultaneously — not sacrifice one for another.
DocsFuzzy.finalScoreFormula: Each sub-score contributes equally. The combination with the highest Optimization Score is selected as The Winner.
DocsFuzzy.finalScoreNote: The Winner is the parameter combination (Acceleration + Weakening Point) that best balances all three metrics according to the fuzzy evaluation. It is the system's recommendation for the optimal train operating specification for this route.

electrical.title: Electrical Parameter Templates
electrical.desc: Download templates for static electrical settings and speed-indexed characteristic curves. For curve CSVs: Column 1 is the speed threshold (km/h) and Column 2 is the corresponding electrical value.
electrical.generalDesc: Standard key-value preset for all scalar electrical parameters (used when no speed-curve CSV is uploaded). The field p_aps is always a scalar and cannot be replaced by a CSV.
electrical.generalCardDesc: Fields: stat_vol_line, stat_vol_motor, stat_pf, stat_eff_gear, stat_eff_motor, stat_eff_vvvf, p_aps
electrical.lineDesc: Two-column CSV. Col 1: Speed threshold (km/h). Col 2: Catenary line voltage (V) at that speed. Required when uploading a CSV for Line Voltage.
electrical.motorDesc: Two-column CSV. Col 1: Speed threshold (km/h). Col 2: VVVF inverter output / motor terminal voltage (V). Required when uploading a CSV for Motor Voltage.
electrical.gearDesc: Two-column CSV. Col 1: Speed threshold (km/h). Col 2: Mechanical gearbox transmission efficiency as a decimal ratio (e.g. 0.92 = 92%). Required when uploading a CSV for Efficiency of Gearbox.
electrical.effMotorDesc: Two-column CSV. Col 1: Speed threshold (km/h). Col 2: Traction motor conversion efficiency as a decimal ratio (e.g. 0.91 = 91%). Required when uploading a CSV for Efficiency of Traction Motor.
electrical.vvvfDesc: Two-column CSV. Col 1: Speed threshold (km/h). Col 2: VVVF inverter conversion efficiency as a decimal ratio (e.g. 0.97 = 97%). Required when uploading a CSV for Efficiency of VVVF.

track.title: Track Parameter Templates
track.desc: Download templates for general track settings and individual environment variables. Each CSV upload field on the Track Parameter page has its own template below.
track.generalDesc: Standard key-value preset for scalar values: number of stations, default radius, slope, speed limit, and dwell time.
track.generalCardDesc: Fields: n_station, x_station, radius, slope, v_limit, dwellTime
track.stationDesc: Three-column CSV defining the inter-station distance segments. Required when uploading a CSV for Station Distances.
track.stationCardDesc: Three-column format: Start, End, Station Distance (m)
track.radiusDesc: Three-column CSV defining the track curve radius per segment. Use 0 for straight sections. Required when uploading a CSV for Radius per Section.
track.radiusCardDesc: Three-column format: Start, End, Radius (m). Use 0 for straight track.
track.slopeDesc: Three-column CSV defining the track gradient. Positive values indicate uphill, negative indicates downhill. Unit is permille (‰). Required when uploading a CSV for Slope per Section.
track.slopeCardDesc: Three-column format: Start, End, Gradient (‰). Negative = downhill.
track.limitDesc: Three-column CSV defining the maximum allowable speed per segment. Required when uploading a CSV for Speed Limit per Section.
track.limitCardDesc: Three-column format: Start, End, Speed Limit (km/h).
track.dwellDesc: Two-column CSV defining the station position (cumulative distance) in meters and its dwell time in seconds. Required when uploading a CSV for Dwell Time at Stations.

running.title: Running Parameter Templates
running.desc: Download the configuration templates for the Running Parameters page.
running.presetDesc: A key-value CSV file containing running constraints such as max speeds, accelerations, and simulation time step.

train.title: Train Parameter Templates
train.desc: Download the configuration templates for the Train Parameters page.
train.presetDesc: A key-value CSV file containing all standard train attributes such as masses, gear ratio, and wheel diameter.
